name: Jiangshi
description: The Jiangshi, also known as a Chinese hopping vampire or zombie, is a reanimated corpse in Chinese folklore. These creatures are said to be created when a person dies with unresolved grievances or is improperly buried. They are characterized by their stiff bodies, rigor mortis preventing them from bending their limbs, forcing them to hop around. Jiangshi are often depicted wearing official Qing dynasty attire, a sign of improper burial practices. They are primarily nocturnal, seeking out living beings to absorb their life force (qi). They are a symbol of decay, bad luck, and the importance of proper funerary rites. The Jiangshi's appearance and behavior have varied across different regions and time periods, but the core concept of a reanimated, hopping corpse remains consistent. They are a popular figure in Chinese horror and fantasy, often depicted as both terrifying and comedic figures. Jiangshi are also associated with certain rituals and talismans designed to subdue or destroy them, highlighting the cultural beliefs surrounding death and the afterlife in Chinese society. They represent the anxieties around unresolved issues and the consequences of neglecting traditional customs.
subtitle: The Hopping Corpse of Chinese Folklore
appearance: Jiangshi are typically depicted as stiff corpses, often clad in the attire of Qing dynasty officials. Their skin is pale or greenish, indicating decomposition. Their eyes are often milky white or bloodshot, and their fingernails are long and sharp. Rigor mortis prevents them from bending their limbs, forcing them to hop with outstretched arms. A paper talisman, often inscribed with Taoist symbols, is frequently seen attached to their forehead to control or suppress them.
habitat: Jiangshi are primarily found in dark, secluded areas, such as cemeteries, caves, and abandoned buildings. They are also said to inhabit areas where improper burials have occurred or where there is a concentration of negative energy. They are most active during the night.
behavior: Jiangshi are driven by a primal instinct to absorb the life force (qi) of living beings. They typically hop towards their victims with outstretched arms, attempting to bite or scratch them. They are often described as being mindless and relentless, driven by hunger and a desire to return to a semblance of life. Jiangshi are sensitive to sunlight and loud noises, which can disorient or weaken them.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Jiangshi
keyMyths.description: Jiangshi are believed to be created when a person dies with unresolved grievances, is improperly buried, or is struck by lightning. These circumstances prevent the soul from peacefully passing on, leading to the reanimation of the corpse. Some legends also attribute the creation of Jiangshi to sorcery or the influence of malevolent spirits.

sources.author: Richard Freeman
sources.description: A compilation of various monster legends from around the world, including a section on Jiangshi.

sources.author: Various Authors
